ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai basil beef; mapped to broader 'beef dish' class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Thai basil beef; A0CKP (beef meat, stir-fried) is the closest defensible parent match based on preparation method and primary ingredient. The analogous nature of this mapping is noted: the specific Thai basil and sauce profile is not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for Thai basil beef stir-fry; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of Thai basil beef (meat only) is approximately 150 g, representing the protein component of a meal when served alongside steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Basil Beef (Meat Only) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Thai basil beef (meat only), cr_id F06040020, with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef stir-fry analogue (USDA SR Legacy #13364) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai basil beef (meat only). GI estimated at approximately 30 based on the analogous category of mixed beef stir-fry dishes with low carbohydrate content (carbohydrate 2.9 g/100g). Lean beef and stir-fried meat dishes with minimal carbohydrate are consistently classified as low-GI foods in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per 150 g serving = (30 × 2.9 × 1.5) / 100 = 1.3, rounded to 0.9 per 100 g equivalent basis. The low carbohydrate content means GI classification has minimal clinical relevance for this food.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 600 mg per 100 g — at the upper boundary of the high-concern threshold, driven by fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce in the stir-fry preparation.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less and request reduced-sauce preparation; pairing with potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or sweet potato may partially offset the sodium load, but this dish should be consumed infrequently as part of a sodium-controlled diet.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 2.9 g per 100 g and sugar 1.27 g per 100 g — the very low carbohydrate content means this dish will not cause significant postprandial glucose elevation when consumed as the protein component of a meal.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thai basil beef (meat only) is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as a high-protein, low-carbohydrate protein source; the primary glycaemic risk comes from accompaniments such as white rice, which should be portioned carefully or substituted with lower-GI alternatives.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 600 mg per 100 g poses a cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation; saturated fat from beef (estimated 2.8 g per 100 g) contributes to LDL cholesterol load, though the overall fat content of 7.51 g per 100 g is moderate.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to small portions (100 g or less), request reduced-sauce preparation to lower sodium, and balance intake with omega-3-rich foods and high-fibre vegetables to partially offset the saturated fat and sodium burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is a primary source of arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid), which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6) directly relevant to RA disease activity; sodium at 600 mg per 100 g may also worsen RA-associated tissue oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise red meat consumption and consider substituting beef with chicken, tofu, or tempeh; chilli (a nightshade) may trigger individual flares and personal tolerance should be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai basil beef (meat only) is a stir-fried Thai dish consisting of thinly sliced or minced beef cooked at high heat with Thai holy basil (kraphao), fresh chilli, garlic, fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce. The dish is the meat-only component of the classic Thai street food Pad Kraphao Neua, served without rice or egg. The beef is tender and savoury with a bold umami flavour profile, moderate heat from chilli, and the distinctive anise-like fragrance of Thai holy basil. Per 100 g, the dish provides 135 kcal, 13.97 g protein, 7.51 g fat, and only 2.9 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate option. Sodium is high at 600 mg per 100 g due to the combination of fish sauce, oyster sauce, and soy sauce. The dish is suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns and is appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes as a protein component, but requires caution for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease due to its sodium content. Thai basil provides polyphenolic antioxidants and garlic contributes allicin, offering modest anti-inflammatory benefit, though these are insufficient to offset the pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid load from red meat for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Thai basil beef?
Is Pad Kraphao Neua suitable for diabetics?
How much sodium is in Thai basil beef?
Can I eat Thai basil beef with hypertension?
Is Thai basil beef high in protein?

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06040020) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy beef stir-fry analogue. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous low-carbohydrate beef dish category. All mandatory Thai-language naming requirements satisfied.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Thai basil beef (meat only) preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F06040020) or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. Saturated fat (2.8 g/100g) estimated from USDA SR Legacy entry #13364 (beef, stir-fried, lean and fat) as the closest approved-source analogue; this value is an approximation and should be updated if HPB publishes a full fatty acid profile. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate based on the low-carbohydrate content (2.9 g/100g) and the University of Sydney GI Database classification of lean beef stir-fry dishes; the low carbohydrate content means GI classification has minimal clinical relevance for this food. The dish is classified as halal based on the input dietary_tags; this assumes halal-certified fish sauce and oyster sauce are used in preparation. Hypertension suitability is classified as high concern because sodium is exactly at 600 mg/100g, the hard gate threshold. FoodEx2 match type set to 'closest_parent' as the valid enum value; the analogous nature of the A0CKP mapping is documented in foodex2_gap_note.
